results.title_inferred: Building Stones
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
De las piedras para fábricas

Toda suerte de piedra franca y berroqueña que es suave de labrar es la que decimos ser a propósito para edificios de cantería, de la cual hay escogidas canteras en todas estas Indias; unas piedras se hallan muy livianas y cavernosas, a modo de un panal o piedra pómez, que no se hunde en el agua, la cual es maravillosa para fábricas, señaladamente para murallas y fortalezas; semejantes a ellas hay otros muchos géneros de piedras.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
On Building Stones

Every sort of freestone [piedra franca] and granite [berroqueña] that is easy to work is what we call suitable for masonry construction [edificios de cantería], of which there are choice quarries throughout these Indies. Some stones are found to be very light and porous, in the manner of a honeycomb or pumice stone [piedra pómez], which does not sink in water; this stone is marvelous for construction, most notably for walls and fortifications. Similar to these there are many other kinds of stones.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: freestone (fine-grained, easily worked sedimentary or calcareous stone)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Spanish architectural term for a soft, workable building stone, typically limestone or fine sandstone.

entities_mentioned.name: berroqueña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: granite
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish term commonly used for granite or hard crystalline stone; contrast with the softer piedra franca.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra pómez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: pumice (volcanic stone)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly compares the lightweight, porous building stones of the Indies to pumice, noting their characteristic of floating on water. These may refer to local volcanic tuffs or similar lightweight vesicular stones found in the Andes region.

La piedra bezar está compuesta de unas escamas o costras como cáscaras de bellotas, unas sobre otras, de las cuales la primera es muy lisa y lustrosa. results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On the Bezoar Stone

The bezoar stone is composed of certain scales or crusts resembling acorn shells, layered one upon another, of which the outermost is very smooth and lustrous. These stones are found in various sizes, ranging from as small as chickpeas up to the size of a hen's egg; and some (though rare) are as large as a fist and weigh a pound or more (20). Their shape is highly varied: some are round; others are oval and flat, and of so many di[fferent forms]...

entities_mentioned.name: piedra bezar (bezoar stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bezoar — a calcified concretion found in the gastrointestinal tract of ruminants (particularly Capra aegagrus and related species in the Old World; various Andean camelids such as vicuña and guanaco in the New World)
entities_mentioned.notes: The bezoar stone was one of the most prized medicinal substances of the 16th–17th centuries, used as a universal antidote and alexipharmic. Cobo here describes its physical structure (concentric laminar layers), size range (from chickpea to hen's egg, occasionally fist-sized), and varied morphology. The passage is cut off mid-sentence before medical uses or preparation methods are detailed. No preparation method is recoverable from this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Mercury Amalgamation Process for Silver Refining
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIX
Del beneficio con azogue

El beneficio de azogue es de mucha más riqueza que el de fundición, porque es más copioso y general y se saca con él todo la plata de los metales, por bajos y pobres que sean. Cuando el metal acude de dos onzas para arriba por quintal, se puede beneficiar con ganancia, y de aquí para abajo, es con muy poca o ninguna, particularmente en los asientos de minas que no tienen indios de cédula y repartimiento, o de mita, como llaman en el Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On the Mercury [Amalgamation] Process

The mercury [amalgamation] process [beneficio de azogue] yields far greater wealth than smelting [fundición], because it is more abundant and universal, and with it all the silver is extracted from the ores [metales], however low-grade and poor they may be. results.translation:
CHAPTER XLII
On Copper

Greater quantities of copper mines are found in this kingdom [Peru] and in many other parts of the Indies than of silver or of other metals. For, lacking iron, they forged from this metal their weapons, tools, and instruments for agriculture and various other trades; however, they made no use of it in any application of medicine. results.title_inferred: On Coca: Description of the Plant and Its Trade
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX
De la coca

En este reino del Perú no hay cosa más conocida que la coca, cuyo trato es de los gruesos y de mayor ganancia que hay en las Indias, y con que no pocos españoles se han hecho ricos. Es la coca una mata no mayor que los manzanos enanos de España, de hasta un estado en alto; su hoja, que es la que tanto precian y estiman los indios, es del tamaño y talle de la del limoncillo, y a veces menor. Da una frutilla colorada, seca y sin jugo, tamaña como pequeños escaramujos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On Coca

In this kingdom of Peru there is no commodity [cosa] better known than coca, the trade in which is among the largest and most profitable to be found in the Indies, and by which no few Spaniards have made themselves rich. Coca is a shrub [mata] no larger than the dwarf apple trees of Spain, reaching up to one estado [approximately six feet] in height. Its leaf, which is the part so prized and esteemed by the Indians, is the size and shape of that of the small lemon tree [limoncillo], and sometimes smaller. It produces a small red fruit, dry and without juice, about the size of small rose hips [escaramujos].

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes the plant morphology: a shrub roughly six feet tall, with leaves similar in size and shape to those of a small citrus tree, and small dry red fruits resembling rose hips. He emphasizes its enormous commercial importance in colonial Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: limoncillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A small-leafed citrus species, likely Citrus aurantiifolia (lime) or a related dwarf variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point for the size and shape of the coca leaf, not as a medicinal or trade entity in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: escaramujos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosa canina (dog rose) hips, or rose hips generally
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the coca fruit; escaramujo is the standard Spanish term for rose hip.

CHAPTER VI

On the Mamón

On the island of Hispaniola they call mamón another tree and fruit resembling the anona [custard apple]. The mamón is a medium-sized tree, as large as a peach tree [durazno], and of very handsome appearance; its leaf is similar to that of the alder [aliso], somewhat larger and more channeled [acanalada], and of a very light green that greatly beautifies the tree. The fruit bears the same name; when it is small and green, it resembles an artichoke [alcachofa] on account of its very uneven surface, as though composed of green scales layered one upon another.

On the Zapotes.

CAPITULO XXI

De los hijos de Manco-Inca que en Vilcabamba mantuvieron el título de reyes

Quedó en Vilcabamba con la borla y título de rey Sayri-Tupac-Inca; y como acá fuera se siguieron las guerras civiles de Gonzalo Pizarro, no se atendió ni a reducirlo ni hacer la guerra, hasta que, sosegada la tierra con la victoria que alcanzaron en Jaquijaguana los leales el año de 1548, el presidente Pedro de la Gasca, deseando dejar la tierra...
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXI

Concerning the sons of Manco Inca who maintained the title of kings in Vilcabamba

Sayri Tupac Inca remained in Vilcabamba bearing the [borla] [the royal fringe or tassel, symbol of Inca sovereignty] and the title of king; and since, in the outside world, the civil wars of Gonzalo Pizarro continued, no effort was made either to reduce him [i.e., bring him into submission] or to wage war against him, until, after the land was pacified by the victory that the loyalists won at Jaquijaguana in the year 1548, President Pedro de la Gasca, desiring to leave the land [at peace]...

results.title_inferred: How the Incas Resettled Conquered Peoples into Towns and the Order They Imposed
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV

Que los Incas reducían a pueblos las gentes que sujetaban, y el orden que en ellos ponían

Ya queda dicho arriba cómo los indios peruanos, antes de ser gobernados por los reyes Incas, no tenían pueblos concertados con traza y forma de nuestros pueblos, sino que vivían en caseríos de poca vecindad, puestos los más por los cerros y collados, para defenderse de los asaltos que unos a otros se daban. Digo ahora, que en poniendo el Inca debajo de su domin[io]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV

On how the Incas resettled into towns the peoples they subjugated, and the order they established among them

It has already been stated above how the Peruvian Indians, before being governed by the Inca kings, did not have organized towns laid out in the manner and form of our own towns, but rather lived in small hamlets of sparse population, situated for the most part on hills and ridges, in order to defend themselves from the raids that they launched upon one another. I say now that upon the Inca bringing [a people] under his domin[ion — text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu), pre-Columbian Andean civilization
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as 'reyes Incas' (Inca kings); the passage discusses their policy of reducción — the forced resettlement of subject peoples into planned towns.

entities_mentioned.name: indios peruanos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous peoples of Peru and the broader Andean region
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic colonial term for the native inhabitants of Peru prior to and during Inca rule.

entities_mentioned.name: caseríos
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish term for small, scattered hamlets or rural settlements; used here in contrast to formally planned colonial-style towns (pueblos). No modern identification applicable — this is a settlement-type descriptor.

The word 'redncíajt' in the chapter heading is an OCR error for 'reducían' (they resettled/reduced), referring to the Spanish colonial policy of reducción, by which indigenous populations were consolidated into nucleated towns for purposes of taxation, labor extraction, and evangelization. Cobo is here attributing an analogous pre-colonial policy to the Incas. The phrase 'traza y forma de nuestros pueblos' ('the layout and form of our towns') is a culturally loaded comparison to Spanish urban planning norms. No medicinal or botanical content is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Laws and Punishments by Which the Incas Governed Their Kingdom
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVI

De las leyes y castigos con que los Incas gobernaban su reino

Como los indios carecían de letras, no tenían leyes escritas, mas conservaban las que habían establecido sus reyes por tradición y con el uso y observancia en que estaban. Las mas principales y de que más memoria había entre ellos pondré aquí.

Donde se hallaba presente el Inca, él solo era el juez ante quien se pedían todos los agravios hechos, y donde no, hacían justicia sus gobernadores y ca[ciques]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVI

On the laws and punishments by which the Incas governed their kingdom

As the Indians lacked letters, they had no written laws; rather, they preserved those which their kings had established through tradition and through the use and observance in which they were maintained. I will set down here the most important ones, those of which the greatest memory existed among them.

Wherever the Inca was present, he alone was the judge before whom all grievances were brought; and where he was not present, justice was administered by his governors and ca[ciques]

entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'Inca' here specifically to denote the sovereign ruler, not the ethnic group broadly. He describes the Inca as the sole judge when personally present.

results.title_inferred: Division of Domesticated Livestock Among the Inca, Religion, and Community
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIX

Del orden cómo se repartía el ganado manso, y la renta que en él y en la ropa de sus lanas tenían el Inca y la religión; y cómo los cazaderos y montes eran realengos

La misma división tenía hecha el Inca de todo el ganado manso que déllas tierras, aplicando una parte a la religión, a sí otra y a la comunidad otra: y no sólo dividió y separó cada una destas partes, sino también las dehesas y pastos en que se apacentasen, de modo que anduviesen e[...]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX

On the manner in which domesticated livestock was divided, and the income that the Inca and the [Catholic] religion derived from it and from the cloth made of its wool; and how the hunting grounds and forests were crown lands [realengos]

The Inca had made the same division of all the domesticated livestock of those lands, assigning one portion to religion, another to himself, and another to the community. And not only did he divide and separate each of these portions, but also the pastures [dehesas] and grazing lands in which they were to be kept, so that they might go about [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca — ruler of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the sovereign Inca ruler as the authority who organized the tripartite division of state resources.

entities_mentioned.name: ganado manso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Domesticated camelids — llamas (Lama glama) and alpacas (Vicugna pacos)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'ganado manso' (tame/domesticated livestock) in Andean colonial contexts invariably refers to llamas and alpacas, not Old World cattle. Their wool ('lanas') was used for textile production ('ropa'), a key state resource.

entities_mentioned.name: religión
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Catholic Church / colonial ecclesiastical establishment, or possibly the Inca state religion (sun worship) in the pre-conquest context
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo likely describes the pre-Hispanic Inca tripartite system (sun/religion, Inca/state, community/ayllu) while framing it in Spanish colonial religious vocabulary. Context suggests the Inca religious cult rather than Catholicism.

entities_mentioned.name: dehesas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Designated pasture lands / grazing commons
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish term for enclosed or designated pasture; used here to translate the Andean concept of assigned grazing territories for each of the three divisions of livestock.

entities_mentioned.name: cazaderos y montes realengos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crown hunting reserves and royal forests
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'realengos' denotes lands belonging to the crown (here, the Inca). The concept parallels European royal game preserves and is applied by Cobo to Inca-controlled hunting grounds for wild camelids (vicuñas, guanacos).

results.title_inferred: The Royal Roads Built by the Incas Across Their Kingdom
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI

De los caminos que hicieron los Incas por todo su reino, y la contribución de gente que para aderezarlos daban las provincias

Tenían hechos los Incas dos caminos reales que tomaban todo el largo de su reino desde la provincia de Quito hasta el reino de Chile, que son novecientas leguas, el uno por los Llanos y costa de la mar, y el otro la tierra adentro por las provincias de la Sierra, desviado de la costa por unas partes treinta leguas y por otras cinc[uenta]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI

Of the roads that the Incas built throughout their kingdom, and the contribution of labor [lit. people] that the provinces provided for their maintenance

The Incas had constructed two royal roads [caminos reales] that ran the entire length of their kingdom, from the province of Quito to the kingdom of Chile—a distance of nine hundred leagues—one running along the Lowlands [Llanos] and the seacoast, and the other going inland through the provinces of the Sierra [highland region], set back from the coast in some places by thirty leagues and in others by fif[ty].

results.title_inferred: Additional Personal-Service Tribute Owed by Indians to Their King
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIII

Del demás tributo que en servicio personal pagaban a su rey los indios

Allende del trabajo que en lugar de pecho o tributo ponían los pueblos en las labranzas y crianzas de las tierras y ganados del Inca y de la religión y en los otros oficios y tareas que habemos contado, era grandísima la contribución que daban de gente y peones para cuantos ministerios y obras se hacían en todo el reino, así para servicio y utilidad del rey, como de la república. A los cuales
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII

On the further tribute that the Indians paid to their king in personal service

Beyond the labor that the towns contributed in lieu of a head-tax [pecho] or tribute — namely the work performed on the farmlands and livestock holdings of the Inca and of the [state] religion, and in the other trades and tasks we have already described — immense was the contribution they rendered in manpower and laborers [peones] for every ministry and public work carried out throughout the entire kingdom, both for the service and benefit of the king and for the commonwealth [república]. To whom [the passage continues]

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca / ruler of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers both to the sovereign ruler and, by extension, to crown lands and holdings.

entities_mentioned.name: pecho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Head-tax or capitation tax (Spanish colonial fiscal term)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Spanish term for a direct personal tax; Cobo uses it to analogize the Andean labor-tribute system to familiar Iberian fiscal categories.

entities_mentioned.name: peones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unskilled or general laborers (mit'a workers in Andean context)
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely corresponds to mit'a laborers obligated to perform state corvée labor under the Inca system.

results.translator_notes: The source text is fragmentary — it ends mid-sentence ('A los cuales'), indicating the segment cuts off before the chapter continues. Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'jxígaban' → 'pagaban', 'bs' → 'las', 'i;' → 'y', 'm' → 'en', 'kcíón' → 'contribución', 'pila' → 'para', 'la¬ / ma' → 'hacían', 'habemos' is a period-authentic first-person plural (= 'hemos'). No botanical, pharmaceutical, or medical content is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Distribution of Labor Tribute (Mita) Among Caciques
results.original_text: Inca y a gobernadores parecía; y la distribución hacían los caciques inferiores a rata por cantidad, como, si habían de sacar mil hombres, contribuía cada cacique con el número que le cabía; de manera que, sabida la gente que había de salir de mito, no podían los pueblos recibir ningún agravio en la distribución ni ser más cargados unos que otros. Y aunque los ganados todos de que daban tributo eran del Inca y de la religión, también hacían de la mi-
results.translation: the Inca and to the governors seemed appropriate; and the distribution was carried out by the lesser caciques on a proportional basis, so that if, for example, one thousand men were to be levied, each cacique contributed the number that fell to his share; in such a way that, once the number of people who were to go out on mita [rotary labor draft] was known, the towns could suffer no injury in the distribution, nor could some be burdened more than others. And although all the livestock that was rendered as tribute belonged to the Inca and to the [state] religion, they also made use of the mi- [text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca — ruler of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the sovereign ruler as the supreme authority over tribute allocation.

entities_mentioned.name: caciques
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Local indigenous lords / curacas
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish colonial term for indigenous regional and local leaders responsible for administering labor quotas within their communities.

entities_mentioned.name: mito / mita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mit'a — Andean rotary labor obligation system
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR renders 'mito' but the intended word is 'mita' (mit'a), the institutionalized Inca system of compulsory rotary labor tribute. Later adopted and adapted by the Spanish colonial administration.

entities_mentioned.name: ganados
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Livestock (likely llamas and alpacas)
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Andean context, tribute livestock would overwhelmingly have consisted of camelids (llamas, alpacas) rather than Old World livestock.

results.translator_notes: The segment is mid-sentence at both beginning and end, indicating it is a fragment of a larger passage. The word 'mito' in the source is a period Spanish rendering of the Quechua 'mit'a'; rendered as 'mita' with bracketed gloss. The final word 'mi-' is a clear mid-word line break cut off by the segment boundary — likely 'mita' or 'misma' in the continuation. The OCR artifact '&' has been silently corrected to 'y' (i.e., 'pueblos'). No medical or botanical content is present in this segment.

Otrosí, fue de muy grande importancia a los Incas para sus designios, la reputación y estima notable que dellos concibieron los indios, mediante la cual se vino a fundar en esta gente ruda una opinión en que no solamente los tenían por diferentes de los otros hombres en valor y fuerzas, mas creían que tuviesen gran paren[tesco con lo divino]

Furthermore, it was of very great importance to the Incas for their designs [political ambitions], the notable reputation and esteem which the Indians conceived of them, through which there came to be established among these simple [rude/uneducated] people an opinion by which they not only held them to be different from other men in valor and strength, but also believed them to have great kin[ship with the divine]

entities_mentioned.name: Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca rulers/nobility of the Andean empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: The passage discusses the political and religious authority of the Inca rulers and how they cultivated a reputation of semi-divine status among subject peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: indios

entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo refers to subject peoples of the Inca empire in general terms, characterizing them as 'gente ruda' (simple/uneducated people), reflecting the paternalistic colonial perspective of the author.

results.title_inferred: On the Reckoning of Time, Quipus, and the Counting Methods of the Peruvian Indians
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVII

Del cómputo del tiempo; de los quipos o memoriales y modo de contar que tenían los indios peruanos

Siendo como es el movimiento de los cielos cosa tan admirable, notoria y manifiesta a todas las gentes, no hay ninguna tan bárbara y ruda que, siquiera cuando levanta los ojos...
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII

On the reckoning of time; the quipus or memoranda, and the method of counting used by the Peruvian Indians

The movement of the heavens being, as it is, a thing so admirable, well-known, and manifest to all peoples, there is no nation so barbarous and rude that, at least when it raises its eyes...

entities_mentioned.name: quipos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quipu (khipu) — knotted cord recording devices used by Andean peoples
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the spelling 'quipos'; the term 'memoriales' (memoranda/records) is given as a Spanish gloss. These knotted cords served mnemonic and administrative functions in Inca society. The segment is introductory and cuts off before full description.

entities_mentioned.name: indios peruanos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous peoples of the Andean region, particularly those under Inca administration
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'indios peruanos' as a general term for the native inhabitants of the Viceroyalty of Peru.

results.translator_notes: The segment is incomplete — it breaks off mid-sentence ('cuando levanta los o[jos]'). The word 'rxida' in the OCR source is an obvious OCR error for 'ruda' (rude/rough/uncultured), silently corrected. Similarly, 'tos indios' in the chapter heading is corrected to 'los indios'. No plant, drug, or medical content is present in this segment; it is an introductory passage on Andean timekeeping and recording methods.

results.title_inferred: Indigenous Methods of Reckoning Time in Peru
results.original_text: s maneras: la una, que señalaban con el dedo la parte del cielo en que andaba el sol cuando comenzaron su obra; como si al caminante le preguntaban a qué hora había salido de la posada, respondía levantando el dedo al cielo y apuntando el paraje donde estaba el sol entonces, por donde sacaban poco más o menos el tiempo que había caminado; y lo mismo hacían en las demás faenas y ejercicios. La otra era muy calificada, desta suerte: casi en todo este reino del Perú cogen los indios cierta
results.translation: ...in two ways: the first was that they would point with their finger to the part of the sky where the sun had been when they began their work; just as if one were to ask a traveler at what hour he had set out from his inn, he would respond by raising his finger to the sky and pointing to the place where the sun had been at that moment, from which one could reckon more or less how long he had been traveling; and they did the same with respect to their other tasks and occupations. The second [method] was a more refined one, of this sort: throughout nearly all of this kingdom of Peru, the Indians gather a certain [text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: reino del Perú (Kingdom of Peru)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colonial-era Peru, broadly encompassing the Andean region of South America
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'reino del Perú' to refer to the Spanish viceroyalty of Peru in its broad colonial extent.

entities_mentioned.name: indios (Indians/indigenous people of Peru)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean indigenous peoples, likely including Quechua-speaking groups among others
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic colonial term for indigenous inhabitants; no single group specified in this passage.

results.translator_notes: This segment is clearly a mid-passage fragment — it begins mid-sentence ('s maneras') and ends mid-sentence ('cogen los indios cierta'), indicating truncation at both ends. The first method described is solar positioning as a folk timekeeping practice. The second method is introduced but cut off before the substance is revealed; likely the next segment describes a plant or agricultural product used in timekeeping or calendrical reckoning. No medical/botanical entities are fully described in this fragment, though the closing phrase ('cogen los indios cierta [cosa/planta]') strongly anticipates a plant description in the continuation.

results.title_inferred: Indigenous opinions and myths concerning the origin of the world, the universal flood, and the resulting sacred shrines (guacas)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO II

De las opiniones y fábulas que acerca de su origen, principios del mundo y diluvio universal estaban recebidas desta gente, con las guacas que desto resultaron

Como el total fundamento de toda religión y culto divino sea el conocimiento de la primera causa, ora sea la verdadera, ora falsa y fingida, de quien los hombres creen haber procedido y depender della en la conservación de su ser, y también la noticia del fin y estado que después desta vida esperan tener; por
results.translation:
CHAPTER II

Concerning the opinions and fables received among these people regarding their origin, the beginnings of the world, and the universal flood, together with the sacred shrines [guacas] that resulted therefrom

Since the total foundation of all religion and divine worship is the knowledge of the first cause — whether true, or false and invented — from whom men believe themselves to have proceeded and upon whom they depend for the preservation of their being, and likewise the understanding of the end and state they expect to have after this life; for [text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: guacas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: huacas — Andean sacred sites, objects, or shrines
entities_mentioned.notes: From Quechua 'wak'a'; refers to sacred places, objects, or beings venerated in Andean religion. Cobo uses this term to denote shrines or cult objects that arose from mythological traditions about creation and the flood.
